title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who share their views on protecting the environment. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Going Green: Four People on Environmental Awareness

texts.A:
### Sophie, 31, Marketing Manager
[43]I became passionate about the environment after watching a documentary about ocean plastic – I couldn't sleep for days after seeing those images.[/43] Since then, I've made some big lifestyle changes. I switched to a plant-based diet, stopped buying fast fashion, and I refuse to use single-use plastic whenever I can. [47]The hardest part has been convincing friends and colleagues that these changes are worth making.[/47] People often think going green means giving something up, but I actually feel I've gained a lot – more awareness, more purpose, and honestly more money, since I spend less on things I don't need.
texts.B:
### David, 54, Retired Engineer
[44]I spent most of my career building infrastructure, so I understand better than most how difficult it is to change large systems quickly.[/44] That's why I believe individual actions matter but aren't enough on their own. Real change has to come from governments and corporations setting stricter standards. [48]I've written to my local MP several times urging them to support renewable energy policies.[/48] People sometimes dismiss me as pessimistic, but I'd call it realistic. [50]I do recycle carefully and have installed solar panels at home, but I don't pretend these things alone will solve the crisis.[/50]
texts.C:
### Aisha, 22, University Student
I grew up in a coastal town and have watched the beaches change noticeably over my lifetime. [45]For me, the most meaningful way to help has been organising beach clean-up events with my university's environmental society.[/45] We pick up hundreds of bags of rubbish every month and it's both exhausting and incredibly satisfying. [49]What I find most rewarding is seeing people who had no interest in the environment turn up once and then keep coming back.[/49] Getting people involved on a local level seems more powerful than any online campaign, in my opinion. I plan to work in environmental policy after I graduate.
texts.D:
### Leo, 39, Secondary School Teacher
[46]I try to incorporate environmental themes into my lessons as much as possible, whatever subject I'm teaching.[/46] I believe education is the most powerful tool we have. If young people grow up understanding the science and feeling empowered to act, change becomes inevitable. [51]Last year, my students ran a zero-waste campaign in our school which resulted in a 40% reduction in litter on the premises.[/51] I was incredibly proud. [52]The biggest obstacle I face is the lack of time in the curriculum to go as deeply into these issues as I'd like.[/52] The pressure to cover exam content leaves little room for broader discussions about the future of our planet.

questions.question: had their attitude changed by something they watched?
questions.explanation: Sophie became passionate about the environment after watching a documentary about ocean plastic.

questions.question: believes their professional background gives them a realistic understanding of the problem?
questions.explanation: David says his career in infrastructure helps him understand how hard it is to change large systems.

questions.question: takes direct practical action in their local community?
questions.explanation: Aisha organises beach clean-up events with her university's environmental society.

questions.question: uses their job to spread awareness among young people?
questions.explanation: Leo incorporates environmental themes into his lessons whatever subject he is teaching.

questions.question: has found it difficult to persuade others in their immediate circle to change their behaviour?
questions.explanation: Sophie says the hardest part has been convincing friends and colleagues that the changes are worth making.

questions.question: has contacted a public official to push for environmental policy?
questions.explanation: David has written to his local MP several times urging support for renewable energy policies.

questions.question: finds it especially satisfying when previously uninvolved people begin to participate?
questions.explanation: Aisha says the most rewarding thing is seeing people with no prior interest keep coming back after attending once.

questions.question: admits that their own individual actions have a limited overall impact?
questions.explanation: David says he recycles and has solar panels but admits these things alone will not solve the crisis.

questions.question: describes a successful environmental initiative carried out by others they inspired?
questions.explanation: Leo's students ran a zero-waste campaign that reduced litter in the school by 40%.

questions.question: mentions a constraint that prevents them from doing as much as they would like?
questions.explanation: Leo says the pressure to cover exam content leaves little time to explore environmental issues in depth.
